verses.text: Then went Samson to Gaza, and saw there an harlot, and went in unto her. It was told the Gazites, saying, Samson is come hither. They compassed him in, and laid wait for him all night in the gate of the city, and were quiet all the night, saying, In the morning, when it is day, we shall kill him. Samson lay till midnight, and arose at midnight, and took the doors of the gate of the city, and the two posts, and went away with them, bar and all, and put them upon his shoulders, and carried them up to the top of an hill that is before Hebron. Guidance: A judge of Israel walks into a Philistine brothel at dusk and walks out at midnight with the city gates. The narrator gives no commentary; the absurdity tells itself. The gates of Gaza weighed many hundreds of pounds — Samson carries them forty miles uphill to Hebron, a city of Judah, as a trophy. The man with extraordinary gift uses it for one more comic gesture instead of for sustained obedience. The Spirit's strength is real; the man's heart is in the wrong town.

It came to pass afterward, that he loved a woman in the valley of Sorek, whose name was Delilah. The lords of the Philistines came up unto her, and said unto her, Entice him, and see wherein his great strength lieth, and by what means we may prevail against him, that we may bind him to afflict him: and we will give thee every one of us eleven hundred pieces of silver. Guidance: 'He loved' — the first time Samson is said to love a woman. The Hebrew word is unguarded; the chapter will reveal he loved her more than her trade or her loyalty deserved. The five Philistine lords pool a bounty of 5,500 shekels — for comparison, a household servant was worth about 30 shekels (Exod 21:32). Delilah is offered a fortune to discover what no army has been able to. The chapter is a setup so visible that the only mystery is why Samson does not see it.

Delilah said to Samson, Tell me, I pray thee, wherein thy great strength lieth, and wherewith thou mightest be bound to afflict thee. Samson said unto her, If they bind me with seven green withs that were never dried, then shall I be weak, and be as another man. Then the lords of the Philistines brought up to her seven green withs which had not been dried, and she bound him with them. Now there were men lying in wait, abiding with her in the chamber. She said unto him, The Philistines be upon thee, Samson. And he brake the withs, as a thread of tow is broken when it toucheth the fire. So his strength was not known. Guidance: The chapter is composed of four rounds. In each, Delilah asks the same question (point-blank), Samson gives a lie that gets a little closer to the truth, and she springs the trap. Round one: seven fresh bowstrings. Why does he not just leave when 'the Philistines be upon thee' becomes the first line of her morning routine? The chapter never explains. Pride, perhaps — the gift man's confidence that no one can really catch him. The narrator's repetition is the warning the man inside the story will not hear.

Delilah said unto Samson, Behold, thou hast mocked me, and told me lies: now tell me, I pray thee, wherewith thou mightest be bound. He said unto her, If they bind me fast with new ropes that never were occupied, then shall I be weak, and be as another man. Delilah therefore took new ropes, and bound him therewith, and said unto him, The Philistines be upon thee, Samson. And there were liers in wait abiding in the chamber. He brake them from off his arms like a thread. Guidance: Round two. 'Thou hast mocked me, and told me lies.' She is unbothered. Liars often pretend to be wounded by lies. Samson tries new ropes — closer to the Lehi miracle (15:13) and closer to revealing something real. Same trap, same shout, same easy victory. But each round wears the secret down. Lust does not arrive at honesty in one step; it arrives one half-truth at a time.

Delilah said unto Samson, Hitherto thou hast mocked me, and told me lies: tell me wherewith thou mightest be bound. He said unto her, If thou weavest the seven locks of my head with the web. And she fastened it with the pin, and said unto him, The Philistines be upon thee, Samson. And he awaked out of his sleep, and went away with the pin of the beam, and with the web. Guidance: Round three involves the hair. Samson is now telling a half-truth — his hair is the real secret, but the trick (weaving it into a loom) is not. He is letting her get warm. The pin and the web give us an interior scene: the loom set up in her chamber, the locks of hair woven in, the warning shout, the man waking and walking away with the whole apparatus dragging behind. Comic and pathetic at once. He is treating his vow like a parlor trick.

She said unto him, How canst thou say, I love thee, when thine heart is not with me? thou hast mocked me these three times, and hast not told me wherein thy great strength lieth. It came to pass, when she pressed him daily with her words, and urged him, so that his soul was vexed unto death; that he told her all his heart, and said unto her, There hath not come a razor upon mine head; for I have been a Nazarite unto God from my mother's womb: if I be shaven, then my strength will go from me, and I shall become weak, and be like any other man. Guidance: Now she uses the weapon that broke him at Timnah — daily tears about love. 'How canst thou say, I love thee, when thine heart is not with me?' The same accusation a Philistine wife once made (14:16). The man learned nothing. 'Vexed unto death' — the Hebrew is the same phrase used of Jonah's despair. He gives in not because she has discovered something but because she has worn his soul down. He tells her 'all his heart' — not a half-truth this time, the whole vow. Pillow talk crosses the line vows used to guard.

When Delilah saw that he had told her all his heart, she sent and called for the lords of the Philistines, saying, Come up this once, for he hath shewed me all his heart. Then the lords of the Philistines came up unto her, and brought money in their hand. She made him sleep upon her knees; and she called for a man, and she caused him to shave off the seven locks of his head; and she began to afflict him, and his strength went from him. Guidance: 'She made him sleep upon her knees' — a maternal-looking pose, made grotesque by what it is for. A barber arrives. The seven locks of the Nazirite vow fall to the floor while Samson sleeps in a woman's lap. He is now ritually defiled in body and naked of the symbol the LORD's angel had spelled out to his mother thirty years before. 'His strength went from him' — not in a single moment of cutting, but in the gradual erosion of every vow that brought him to this couch.

When Delilah saw that he had told her all his heart, she sent and called for the lords of the Philistines, saying, Come up this once, for he hath shewed me all his heart. Then the lords of the Philistines came up unto her, and brought money in their hand. She made him sleep upon her knees; and she called for a man, and she caused him to shave off the seven locks of his head; and she began to afflict him, and his strength went from him. The seven locks of the Nazirite vow fall to the floor while Samson sleeps in a woman's lap. 'His strength went from him' — not in a single moment of cutting, but in the gradual erosion of every vow that brought him to this couch.

She said, The Philistines be upon thee, Samson. He awoke out of his sleep, and said, I will go out as at other times before, and shake myself. And he wist not that the LORD was departed from him. Guidance: The saddest verse in Judges. 'I will go out as at other times before, and shake myself.' He thinks the gift is automatic, his to reach for whenever needed. 'And he wist not that the LORD was departed from him.' The most terrifying possibility in scripture — losing God's presence without noticing. The Nazirite vow had three signs (no wine, no corpse, uncut hair); Samson had broken the first two long ago. The hair was the last visible marker, and once it was gone the Spirit Himself withdrew. The gift is not the man; the gift is the Giver. When the Giver leaves, the man finds out only when he tries to flex and nothing comes.

But the Philistines took him, and put out his eyes, and brought him down to Gaza, and bound him with fetters of brass; and he did grind in the prison house. Guidance: Eyes that lusted now see nothing. Hands that tore a lion now turn a mill wheel like an ox. Feet that walked into Philistine cities are chained with bronze. Samson is reduced to a prisoner-animal in the city whose gates he once carried. Every part of the chapter's punishment is a precise mirror of the chapter's sins. God's discipline often reads the man's own life back to him with the verbs rearranged.

Howbeit the hair of his head began to grow again after he was shaven. Guidance: A single quiet verse, but the door to the chapter's climax. The narrator does not say his strength returned with the hair (it will return because of his prayer, v28); the verse simply notes that life continues to grow even on a defeated head. God's mercy moves in living roots when the man cannot yet pray. Sometimes restoration begins before the sinner knows enough to ask for it.

Then the lords of the Philistines gathered them together for to offer a great sacrifice unto Dagon their god, and to rejoice: for they said, Our god hath delivered Samson our enemy into our hand. When the people saw him, they praised their god: for they said, Our god hath delivered into our hands our enemy, and the destroyer of our country, which slew many of us. It came to pass, when their hearts were merry, that they said, Call for Samson, that he may make us sport. They called for Samson out of the prison house; and he made them sport: and they set him between the pillars. Guidance: Dagon is the Philistine grain god. They credit their god with capturing Samson — a theological boast that will be very specifically disproved in fifteen verses. They sing a couplet repeated in v23 and v24 (rhyming in Hebrew), then drag the eyeless judge out to entertain them. 'He made them sport' — perhaps a sad parade, perhaps mock combat. The position 'between the pillars' is set up here as a stage, and will be its own gallows in the final scene. Pride displays its trophies in the wrong room.

Samson said unto the lad that held him by the hand, Suffer me that I may feel the pillars whereupon the house standeth, that I may lean upon them. Now the house was full of men and women; and all the lords of the Philistines were there; and there were upon the roof about three thousand men and women, that beheld while Samson made sport. Guidance: A blind man asks a boy to let him touch the load-bearing columns. The lad does not see the danger; nobody does. The Dagon temple is built on a typical Philistine plan — a main hall with central pillars supporting an upper gallery. Three thousand on the roof; the lords on the main floor; a full house. The architecture is about to become the verdict. Samson, who once made jokes about doors, will now bring down a building.

Samson said unto the lad that held him by the hand, Suffer me that I may feel the pillars whereupon the house standeth, that I may lean upon them. Now the house was full of men and women; and all the lords of the Philistines were there; and there were upon the roof about three thousand men and women, that beheld while Samson made sport. Guidance: A blind man asks a boy to let him touch the load-bearing columns. The lad does not see the danger; nobody does. The Dagon temple is built on a typical Philistine plan — a main hall with central pillars supporting an upper gallery. Three thousand on the roof; the lords on the main floor; a full house. The architecture is about to become the verdict. Samson, who once made jokes about doors, will now bring down a building.

Samson called unto the LORD, and said, O Lord GOD, remember me, I pray thee, and strengthen me, I pray thee, only this once, O God, that I may be at once avenged of the Philistines for my two eyes. Samson took hold of the two middle pillars upon which the house stood, and on which it was borne up, of the one with his right hand, and of the other with his left. Samson said, Let me die with the Philistines. He bowed himself with all his might; and the house fell upon the lords, and upon all the people that were therein. So the dead which he slew at his death were more than they which he slew in his life. Guidance: His last words on earth begin 'O Lord GOD, remember me.' Memory was the old Nazirite-mother's word for everything he had forgotten. He prays three times in two verses ('I pray thee… I pray thee… O God'). The motive is mixed — vengeance for two eyes is still personal. But the petition is finally to the right Person. And God answers. Samson grasps the pillars; bows himself; the house falls. The same God who 'departed' in v20 returns in answer to the cry of v28. Sin separates; prayer reconnects. The hair has grown; the heart has finally aimed itself upward; the strength returns one last time. He dies with his enemies; in death he kills more than in life. The deliverer who 'began' to deliver (13:5) finishes by burying the leadership of Philistia under one roof. He never freed Israel from them entirely; he gave David the chance to.

Then his brethren and all the house of his father came down, and took him, and brought him up, and buried him between Zorah and Eshtaol in the burying place of Manoah his father. And he judged Israel twenty years. Guidance: His brothers come to recover the body. The mighty man who left home demanding 'get her for me' is carried home on someone else's shoulders. He is buried with his father, the man whose careful piety could never restrain his son's appetites. The book repeats his twenty years of judging (echoing 15:20) as if to make sure the obituary is filed. Hebrews 11:32 will still list him among the faithful — proof that the LORD remembers what He chose to do through a man, even if that man has spent most of his life refusing to choose the same things himself.
